title: Cooking Oil on Linen
stainChemistry: Cooking oil (vegetable, olive, canola) contains long-chain triglycerides that penetrate linen rapidly due to low viscosity. Fresh oil is easily dissolved by surfactants. Aged oil oxidizes and polymerizes — especially polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean) which cross-link into a varnish-like film. Heat accelerates this polymerization.
whyThisWorks: Surfactant (Dawn is the consumer standard) breaks the oil into micelles — tiny droplets surrounded by surfactant molecules that water can carry away from linen. For polymerized oil, a solvent pre-treatment dissolves the hardened film before surfactant can work. Avoid heat until the oil is fully removed — it polymerizes the residue.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply POG (Paint Oil Grease remover) directly to the stain. Tamp gently
2. If POG plateaus: apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action
3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify remaining oil micro-droplets. Flush with cool water
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for final pH restoration
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain to absorb surface oil — wait 10 min
2. Brush off powder with a clean cloth
3. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently into fabric
4. Rinse with warm water. Repeat soap application if stain persists
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 treatment rounds. Take to a professional dry cleaner — they have specialized solvents for oil-based stains
warnings: Always test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — linen dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Heat from dryer permanently sets oil residue

neverDo: No strong concentrated acids — weakens cellulose
No chlorine bleach on colored linen
fiberSensitivities: Strongest natural fiber when wet — very durable
Wrinkles easily but tolerates aggressive cleaning
Neutralize acid spotters after use on cellulose

escalation.when: If cooking oil stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is cooking oil on linen, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — linen dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
